questions.question_text: A North Dakota salesperson closes a transaction and wants the seller to pay her directly. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, compensation must flow through her broker
distractors: Yes, if the seller agrees in writing
Yes, for transactions under $10,000
No, but she may accept a gift instead
questions.explanation: In North Dakota, a salesperson may receive compensation ONLY through their designated broker. Direct payment from a client or customer to a salesperson is prohibited regardless of the transaction amount.

questions.question_text: Two North Dakota brokerages discuss commission rates for an upcoming listing. Broker A suggests they both charge 6%. What legal issue does this raise?
distractors: This is standard industry practice
Only the listing broker sets the rate
The Commission sets minimum rates annually
questions.explanation: Real estate commissions in North Dakota are fully negotiable. There are no fixed rates, and any agreement between brokerages to set uniform commission rates could constitute price-fixing, which violates antitrust laws.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks their agent how much the agent will be paid. Under North Dakota rules, what must the agent do?
distractors: Decline to answer as it is confidential
Refer the question to the listing broker
Provide only a general industry estimate
questions.explanation: Compensation must be disclosed in North Dakota. Agents are required to be transparent about their compensation arrangements so clients can make informed decisions about representation.

questions.question_text: A salesperson changes brokerages mid-transaction. From whom does the salesperson receive the commission for the pending deal?
questions.correct_answer: The broker who held the listing
distractors: The new brokerage they transferred to
Directly from the closing attorney
The buyer's lender at closing
questions.explanation: Salespersons receive compensation only through their designated broker. For a pending transaction, the commission flows through the broker under whom the salesperson was working when the transaction originated.

questions.question_text: A North Dakota broker tells a seller that all brokerages in the area charge 6% commission. What is wrong with this claim?
questions.correct_answer: Commissions are fully negotiable by law
distractors: The standard rate is actually 5%
Only the seller sets the commission rate
The Commission publishes approved rates
questions.explanation: There is no standard or fixed commission rate in North Dakota. All commissions are fully negotiable. Claiming a uniform rate exists is both a misrepresentation and potentially an antitrust violation.

questions.question_text: A North Dakota licensee is considering purchasing a listed property for personal investment. What must the licensee disclose?
questions.correct_answer: Their personal interest in the transaction
distractors: Nothing, licensees may buy freely
Only the purchase price to their broker
Their investment strategy to the seller
questions.explanation: A licensee must disclose any personal interest in a transaction, including when they are purchasing property for themselves. This financial interest in the property must be disclosed to all parties involved.

questions.question_text: A listing agent's sister submits an offer on the agent's listing without the seller knowing about the family relationship. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Failure to disclose a party relationship
distractors: No violation if the offer is at full price
A trust account violation
A team advertising violation
questions.explanation: North Dakota licensees must disclose relationships with parties to a transaction. A family relationship between the listing agent and the buyer is a material fact that the seller needs to make an informed decision.

questions.question_text: A licensee represents the buyer in one transaction and the seller in a separate transaction on the same property. What must be disclosed?
questions.correct_answer: The multiple representation to all parties
distractors: Nothing if the transactions are separate
Only the commission earned on each side
The licensee's total annual income
questions.explanation: If a licensee represents more than one party in related transactions, this must be disclosed to all parties. Transparency about multiple representations helps prevent conflicts of interest.

questions.question_text: A licensee owns a 25% share in a property being listed by another agent in the same brokerage. What must the licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the financial interest to all parties
distractors: No disclosure needed within the same firm
Disclose only if asked by the buyer
Disclose only to the Commission after closing
questions.explanation: Any financial interest in a property must be disclosed by the licensee. A 25% ownership stake is a material financial interest that all parties need to know about, regardless of whether the licensee is directly involved in the sale.

questions.question_text: Which of the following must a North Dakota licensee disclose in a transaction?
distractors: Only interests exceeding $10,000
Only interests in commercial properties
Only interests disclosed by other parties
questions.explanation: North Dakota requires licensees to disclose personal interest, relationships, multiple representation, and financial interest in any transaction. There is no minimum dollar threshold for this disclosure requirement.

questions.question_text: A homeowner pays her unlicensed neighbor $500 for helping find a buyer for the property. Is this lawful in North Dakota?
questions.correct_answer: No, only licensed persons may receive such fees
distractors: Yes, if the amount is under $1,000
Yes, homeowners can pay anyone they choose
No, but the neighbor can accept a gift instead
questions.explanation: Only licensed persons may receive compensation for performing real estate services in North Dakota. Paying an unlicensed person for procuring a buyer is a violation of licensing law.

questions.question_text: A North Dakota broker wants to split a referral fee with an unlicensed business consultant who sent her a client. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, fee-splitting with unlicensed persons is barred
distractors: Yes, if the consultant signs a disclosure
Yes, referral fees are exempt from this rule
No, unless the fee is under $250
questions.explanation: North Dakota prohibits fee-splitting with unlicensed persons except as specifically permitted by law. A business consultant without a real estate license cannot receive a portion of the broker's commission.

questions.question_text: A licensed agent at Brokerage A receives a bonus directly from Brokerage B for referring a client. What rule does this violate?
questions.correct_answer: Compensation must come through the agent's own broker
distractors: Referral bonuses are always permitted
Only the amount exceeding $500 is prohibited
The violation is failing to report income
questions.explanation: A salesperson may only receive compensation through their own designated broker. Accepting payment directly from another brokerage bypasses the required broker-to-broker compensation channel.

questions.question_text: An unlicensed property manager collects a finder's fee for connecting a landlord with a tenant for a property sale. Is this lawful?
questions.correct_answer: No, a license is required to earn such fees
distractors: Yes, property managers are exempt
Yes, if the fee is called a consulting fee
No, unless the landlord is also licensed
questions.explanation: Receiving compensation for facilitating a real estate transaction requires a license. Regardless of the title given to the fee, an unlicensed person cannot earn fees for brokerage activities in North Dakota.

questions.question_text: Under North Dakota law, which person may legally receive a real estate commission?
distractors: Any person involved in the transaction
An unlicensed assistant who showed the home
A retired agent with an inactive license
questions.explanation: Only persons holding an active real estate license may receive compensation for real estate services. Unlicensed individuals and those with inactive licenses are prohibited from receiving commissions or referral fees.
